ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general udon noodle dish class; no specific seafood curry udon entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup dish — wheat noodles in broth with seafood and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated seafood curry udon code. A0CKP (noodle soup dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for seafood curry udon as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of seafood curry udon weighs approximately 600 g and constitutes a complete single-serve meal as typically offered at Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Udon noodle soup with seafood and vegetables, composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for seafood curry udon as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked udon noodles (FDC ID 168917), mixed seafood (prawns, squid, clams), Japanese curry roux, and vegetables, weighted by typical recipe proportions. Input per_100g values (energy 125 kcal, protein 5.83 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 14.17 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamins A, B-group, D, E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are not reliably derivable from available approved sources for this composite and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seafood curry udon as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 (low, upper boundary of low GI range ≤55) based on the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked udon noodles (GI ~55, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The standard GI classification used by the University of Sydney GI Database defines low GI as ≤55, medium GI as 56–69, and high GI as ≥70. A GI of exactly 55 therefore falls in the low category. Japanese curry roux and potato components may slightly elevate the effective GI in practice; however, the seafood and broth dilution effect moderates the overall glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 85.02) / 100 = 46.8 ≈ 46.9 g (carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 14.17 × 6 = 85.02 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 350 mg/100 g; a 600 g serving delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl, request reduced curry roux and soy sauce, and omit fish cake to substantially lower the sodium load of this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat udon noodles (GI 55, low category) combined with high-GI potato in curry roux and 14.17 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.0 g dietary fibre results in a high glycaemic load of approximately 46.9 per standard 600 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion, substitute with shirataki or konjac noodles if available, increase the seafood and vegetable ratio, and monitor post-meal blood glucose carefully given the high glycaemic load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 350 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is moderate at 2.0 g/100 g and trans fat is low at 0.03 g/100 g; seafood provides lean protein and some omega-3 fatty acids which are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions due to high cumulative sodium per serving, request omission of fish cake and reduced curry roux, and note that the seafood components (prawns, squid, clams) provide cardioprotective lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids that partially offset the sodium concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Seafood provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; however, fish cake is a processed product with elevated sodium and additives, and potatoes are a nightshade vegetable that may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request omission of fish cake, monitor response to potato (nightshade), and verify that the curry roux does not contain palm oil or other pro-inflammatory fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Curry Udon is a Japanese noodle dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat udon noodles served in a rich, golden Japanese curry broth, generously topped with a variety of seafood including prawns, squid, clams, and fish cake, along with vegetables such as potato, carrot, and green onion. The broth is prepared by simmering dashi stock (made from kombu seaweed and bonito flakes) with Japanese curry roux, which imparts a mild, savory-sweet spiced flavour distinct from South Asian curries. The udon noodles are boiled separately and added to the broth, absorbing the curry flavour while retaining their characteristic soft yet springy texture. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 125 kcal, 5.83 g protein, 5.0 g fat (2.0 g saturated), 14.17 g carbohydrate, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 350 mg sodium. A standard 600 g bowl delivers approximately 750 kcal and 2100 mg sodium, making sodium management the primary clinical concern. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low category per University of Sydney GI Database classification of GI ≤55) based on the udon noodle component, but the glycaemic load per serving is high at approximately 46.9 due to the large carbohydrate mass per bowl. This dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension or type 2 diabetes without significant modification. It is a popular menu item at Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore, often listed as a lunch or dinner option.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for seafood curry udon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for component ingredients (cooked udon noodles, mixed seafood, Japanese curry roux, vegetables) weighted by typical recipe proportions. The input per_100g values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained. GI is estimated from the analogous udon noodle GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite seafood curry udon dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024 for this specific preparation. All core macros (protein_g 5.83, fat_g 5.0, carbohydrate_g 14.17) are non-zero and consistent with the composite dish profile; no zero-macro justification is required. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.03 g/100 g (trace level from curry roux). The ethnic_tags in the source input ('Sushi', 'Tempura', 'Grilled Fish') are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded. The country_of_origin field in the source input was listed as Singapore, reflecting the serving context; the cuisine origin is correctly attributed to Japan. gi_category is set to 'low' because the University of Sydney GI Database classification defines low GI as ≤55, medium GI as 56–69, and high GI as ≥70; a GI of exactly 55 therefore falls in the low category. Aliases consistency check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent' as per G5 requirements.

Iteration 2: Added mandatory local names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to local_names and aliases_by_language, and added these to the flat aliases array to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 for a dish served in Singapore.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with University of Sydney GI Database classification (low GI ≤55, medium GI 56–69, high GI ≥70); GI value of exactly 55 falls in the low category. Updated gi_source notes and llm_training description to reflect this correction. Updated notes field to document the gi_category rationale.
